speaker: interviewer
text: And then, Willy, I'll get you to grab this slate. And when I let-- you know, I'll let you know, and you can-- if you just want to hold it in front of Leo and-- in a second?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Yael Hirsch. The date is July the 14th, 1966. I'm interviewing Leopold Rosner in Melbourne, Australia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I meant that to-- 1996. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Leopold Rosner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-E-O-P-O-L-D Rosner, R-O-S-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poldek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Leopold is short Leo-- Leopold, Poldek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was called in Poland, Poldek. And on papers, I got Leopold. But when I came to Australia, they cut it to Leo.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1918 on 26 of June. That makes me 78 years young. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Cracow in Poland-- and a large musical family. My father was a violinist. And my brother was a violinist. My other brother was a pianist. And everybody plays some instrument. There was nine children and parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Henry or Chaim born, but to go on the Henry name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who taught your father the violin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who taught your father the violin?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was self-taught.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we played together with my father in some birthdays, some restaurants, some pubs. I started when I was 15 year old. And then, eventually, I started with my brothers. I play in nightclubs, in restaurants. I play in nightclubs from-- in Warsaw from 5 o'clock till 8:00, and then from 9 o'clock till 6 o'clock morning every night of the year except Easter and Christmas. So the whole summer, I haven't seen night. And the whole winter, I haven't seen daylight. But I survived somehow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was your brother that you played with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother did you play with?

speaker: interviewee
text: It, it would-- both with Henry, sometime with both brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the names of your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: One brother is Henry-- Herman Rosner, and other one, George, or Jerzy, Rosner. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] George lives in America still. He went before the war to America. He was engaged to play international fair in '39. He left his wife and child in Poland. He's supposed to come back in September. Then war broke out. He learned that they killed his wife and child. So he didn't had what for to come to Poland. So he stay in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had plenty of brothers. I had, first of all, Regina, my sister, the oldest one. She was with us in concentration camp. And she died after the war. Then Henry-- Herman, the violinist, he was second oldest. He was with me in concentration camp. And he died also six months ago in America. Then brother George, pianist, who lives still in America-- great pianist. Then was sister Mary or Mirysia, a very intelligent girl. She spoke five languages before the war. She played piano. She sang. And she died during the war. They took a group of women, Jewish women, on the boat and sank the boat in middle of the sea in Stutthof. Then I had a young-- another sister, Mela-- Melanie. She was a very good accordionist. She was married. And when the-- in ghetto in Cracow was a segregation. She didn't look very strong physically. Well, they put her to the left, where she was holding her little boy of two and a half year in her arm. One German grabbed the child by legs and hit against the wall. I never seen them again. Then was one year older sister Bronia-- Bronislawa. She died before the war tragically. She fall, by accident, into big pot with boiling water. And then am I the next one, and then younger brother Sam or Samek, a very good violinist. He was with us in Plaszow. And he went to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Dachau-- not Dachau. I think Dachau. And he died one day before the end of the war from hunger. Then the youngest brother [? Willek ?] or Bill, he's alive still in America. So from nine children, all what left is two brothers in America. And I'm here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was Freidel or [? Francesca ?] Grunberg from-- born. A little--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grunberg-- G-R-- it was spelled with, with umlaut. This is German. So U with two line over, you read it E. G-R-U-N-B-E-R-G, Grunberg. She was a little woman. And she had nine children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live in Cracow before the beginning of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't-- we didn't come from-- we haven't been rich. It is such a big family. We had only a big kitchen and big room. In that big room was a great piano-- grand piano. And my parents slept in bed. And I think I slept with them. Another bed, two sisters slept. And two younger brother, one slept on the piano, one under the piano, [LAUGHS] typical musical background. And my older brother usually were interstate or overseas. See, such a big family, so I had to start to work to upkeep the family. So I didn't have time to study to go to school. I had just elementary state school. And I had to work first with my father and then with my brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where, where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow, in Warsaw, in Zakopane, in Krynica, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in all different town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your first professional engagement?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't have a clue. See, I was-- I was very, very young. But I had to wear dinner suit and atmosphere and all the smoke and drunkards. Some better restaurants was-- I would work in Warsaw in Adria, one of the best restaurant. In-- in Lodz, very big restaurant, well known.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Casanova in Lodz. I beg your pardon-- [? Tabarin, ?] [? Tabarin. ?] Casanova is in Poland. And you didn't work long-- many, many months. And there is usually two or three months. And then you have to change.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play just with your father and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- with, with, with father and brother, never together-- or with my father, or with my brothers. See, the last time from the beginning of the war, I play in Cracow with my brother George in [? Wierzynek, ?] one of the best restaurant. And German were already there. One German in uniform-- Polish-- in German uniform, spoke to me perfect Polish to play Polish anthem. I said, I've forgotten. [LAUGHS] Maybe when I play, he would shoot me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play the piano and the accordion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I play piano, accordion, and bass, double bass. I play a little piano, but I play piano, accordion mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe yourself as a religious family-- as a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were, were liberal, but keeping the-- all holidays, Jewish holidays. I was going almost every Friday with my father to synagogue and Saturday. And brother of my father was a gabbai in synagogue. And we had separate pan-- pots for-- in place for meat and for milky products-- quite strict, but semi-religious. My grandfather was quite a religious man.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you celebrate the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry, how--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you celebrate the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had the Pesach all together. Or every Friday, we had a dinner-- family dinner. Always somebody was missing because brother were overseas. One played in Vienna. One played in Berlin before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you travel yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only in Poland-- only in Poland. I had a-- I could have a contract with Josephine Baker in her band but-- didn't want to let me out because I was in the military age. So they didn't want to let me out of Poland. Before the war, I play in two films music and all kind of venues.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you earn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't remember how much. It was quite, quite a good payment. And I-- of course, I give everything to my parents. While we away-- was other state, I sent by post to my parents. I play in Kyrnica very famous place of Jan Keipura, the Polish famous singer. I was five years ago in Poland to see the place. It's changed completely but good memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you on September the 1st, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was in Cracow. And there was-- when German came in-- was a lot of restrictions. And there was a-- on the walls were those restrictions printed. I can read it to you. First of all, 'a census of all Jews in Cracow is taken. A Jewish bank account are closed. Places of worship are closed. Prior gathering in private home are forbidden. The [INAUDIBLE] enlisting Jewish enterprise is issued. Jewish shops must display a sign indicating their Jewish ownership. A white armband with a blue Star of David is to be worn on the right arm. Jewish primary schools are closed. Railway travel is forbidden. Entering restaurants and working in the restaurant is forbidden. Entering parks is forbidden. Sitting on the bench is forbidden. Entering certain street is forbidden. Practicing medicine is forbidden. Practicing law is forbidden.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But on the beginning of the war, I went with my brother to Warsaw to play just a couple weeks before they closed the Warsaw Ghetto. So I came back to Cracow. This is in Warsaw when I play with Eddie Rosner, the famous trumpet player in Warsaw, and my cousin. And I went back to Cracow. And then, after a short time in Cracow, there came order that all Jews have to leave Cracow. So we left to a little town [? Tienits, ?] close to Cracow, with all my family. And up there, I play with my brother for the peasants. I play in a barn. I make a party. For this, we get paid a bag of potatoes or a bag of flour. There we stay about nine months. Then came order that all Jews have to go into ghetto, Cracow Ghetto. To explain what was ghetto, it was a few blocks of houses in [NON-ENGLISH] in Podgorze in Cracow surrounded by barbed wire and some brick wall. There was big entrance-- big way out. There was a tram going through the ghetto but not stopping. And no one was allowed to leave the ghetto. It was police on both ends. And we were being pressed in-- into apartment usually one couple with a child who would be pressed five or six couple or families, some with small children. Terrible cramp-- shout-- children crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you imagine five woman cooking at the same stove? [LAUGHS] But that was only beginning. And it was very, very hot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they start to make segregation. They called one day all people. The older one, they put to the left, the younger one to the right. They took my-- both parents to the left. And I never seen them again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a little cafe in ghetto where I play with my brother, Henry. And this is where I met my wife, Helene. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We decided to get married. We get married on the 17th of January, '43, 1943. And on the night of the wedding, I was taken to concentration camp for honeymoon alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe to me, did you have any documentation in the-- in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any documentation?

speaker: interviewer
text: Documentation.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, only Kennkarte. This is people who work get a stamp. And you are allowed to-- otherwise, if you have-- didn't have a Kennkarte, they took you out. And you would never be seen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- my, my job was going on the guards to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Luftgau Kommando. This is fly-- air, air, air, air Kommando. I was going under guard there. And I had to light fire in 36 iron stoves, 36 every morning. When I came to the 36th, [LAUGHS] the first one was gone. Who were your guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your guards? Your guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: My guard? Germans with, with guns. And they're watching all day. And then at night, we're coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there also Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish police--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there also Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they are called Ordnungsdienst, the Jewish policemens. But I was playing for the ghetto commandant.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my god. Spira-- he was a glazier, hardly could speak Polish. And he was the chief-- chief of police, Jewish police. And they been segregating the policemens also. But because I was a musician, I had a little bit better than others. And so as I mentioned before, there's a restaurant where we play. We had to send every month to propaganda office a list of tuned-- tunes which we intend to play, all names-- operettas, this, and this, and this, and this. And we also wrote a tune, a religious tune-- liturgical-- synagogue tune, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. This is tune about-- translating it dear God, why did you forsake us, something like this. We get a reply-- cross out that tune. And a note-- this is a complain song. The Jews are not allowed to complain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, so on the day of my wedding, I went to the concentration camp to Plaszow, which was on outskirt of Cracow. It was built on, on old Jewish cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who decided when you went to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jewish police had a list of people that with very short notice and-- go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was police. No, the Judenrat is about the same. The Judenrat was above this. They advise us. But there was police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know almost all, all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me some names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't remember now. One was [? Goldberg, ?] who was making monkey business with the list-- Schindler's list, like, taking, you know, bribe-- bribery. But that's-- some were very good. Some [? weren't ?] very good. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when they came to Plaszow, there was camp Commandant Amon Goeth, a terrible murderer, unpredictable person, who was shooting from balcony to people just moving around, like to birdies. And for him, it was much easier to shoot the person than for me to catch a fly. And when they came to ask my profession, I knew that musician is not the profession for concentration camp. So I said, I'm a painter. So with my brother, they put us in a painter shop-- painter barrack. It was about 18 of us. And it was very easy work. We paint a red ribbon on the bucket in case of fire-- red ribbon for [? 'sant' ?] and blue ribbon for water in case of fire. It was quite easy work. But there was another 40-- another 30 painters going under guard out of the concentration camp to work some in Cracow. But one day, wasn't enough work for them that I put them together with us. So from 8-- and become 48. Suddenly, somebody said, Goeth is coming. Everybody was shaking, trembling. He came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: The eldest of the group-- they're called a kapo. Herr Kommandant, 43 inmates. He count. It's 41. Where are two? He said, one is painting in the red kitchen. Another one-- another one, I think, in toilet. You a kapo, you think? Bang! Just on the spot, shot him. It was witnessed by his father-- old father. So here, you don't want to work? I'm going to shoot you all. If somebody can explain you, why don't you want to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody forgot tongue in mouth. Everybody trembling. But my brother spoke good German because he work in Vienna before the war. His wife was Viennese.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he stood up, said, Herr Kommandant. He explained that we are the 18 doing this and this. The other 30 were going out. But there wasn't enough work for them. So that's how it comes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, so, my-- want to work. The other one don't want to work. I'm going to give you all hiding-- strapping with the-- with the leather strap-- 10 on naked bottom. They called Ukrainian guards. And everyone had to pull the pants down, bend on the table, get 10 belts. But believe me, one is enough. And then, put up trousers, say, thank you. And came a row for the old father-- the previously killed son. He said to Ukraine there in Polish, please, not strong. Goeth overheard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what he said? What he said? Oh, he said, slow. So I will do it. He gave him three strokes. He was dead on the table.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So was many such a happening. And Goeth learned that we are musicians. So after some execution to clear his conscience-- although he didn't have a conscience-- he make a big party. He invite all his friends in called us to entertain the people, the guests, his guests.

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been wearing normally blue and white striped suit. But for this occasion, they give us some old dinner suits. And my brother with violin-- I, accordion, we started-- we went under guards to this red house, to his place-- a lot of drunk Germans. And even the German were afraid of Amon Goeth. And on these parties, I meant-- met Oskar Schindler. One German where we've been playing when the commandant wasn't in the room said that the music is even beautiful in barbaric time. One German said this. On one occasion, Oskar Schindler put arm around me. He said, Rosner, don't worry. Everything will be all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe to me what Oskar Schindler looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: He look-- he was very handsome tall. He was chain-smoker. He was drunk. I think he like brandy, good cognacs. He was womanizer. He was a gambler. But I wish more people like him would be. More people would be safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him with Amon Goeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was seen with Amon around his party. He was a good friend of Amon Goeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, best friend-- drinking together. See, Amon Goeth was unpredictable. Once, just for fun, he put a gun to my head. He forced me to drink a liter of vodka, whole liter-- and my brother, which was 14 year older. I play still after this. But my brother-- on the violin, you play vibration by shaking finger, [SINGING] ta. But because of vodka, it was slow motion, played [SINGING] ta. But one good thing-- I've never been drunk since that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any particular evening when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --played?

speaker: interviewee
text: On one occasion, one drunk officer shot above our head with a gun and said, play by Suppe. So we knew the tune. With my brother, we could play about 100,000 from memory plus by-- music. And we played this. Lucky you'll play it or will shoot you. On other occasion, one drunk officer come to ask-- and asked us to play Hungarian tune which was banned before the war in Hungary and Poland. They call this suicidal tune, called 'Gloomy Sunday,' and-- in French, called 'Sombre Dimanche.' And it was beautiful tune about young boy in love with a girl. And she left-- she left him. He commit suicide. He asked us to play the tune. He was a bit drunk. You play once. He asked us to play again, again, and again about six times. And then he went on the balcony. He shoot himself. He commit suicide on the party.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened while you were playing the tune?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, of course. See, that was-- that scene was filmed on the But was cut out because it was too long film-- 3 hour, 15 minutes. And Schindler-- Spielberg sent me this copy of that scene how he commits suicide. And we're going go out. And Schindler say, bye, Leo, bye, Henry. I got the video here.
